meta.project: Vitics — Nigeria AI Energy Intelligence Platform
meta.description: Grounded knowledge entries extracted from Nigerian energy policy documents. Focus: what consumers actually experienced vs what policy promised.

documents.title: Rural Electrification Strategy and Implementation Plan (RESIP)
documents.issuer: Federal Ministry of Power, Works and Housing

policy_promises.promise: Electricity access for 75% of the population (rural and urban)
Electricity access for 90% of the population
Add 1 million rural connections and 800MW of generation capacity
policy_promises.note: Interim target set within the document itself

policy_promises.promise: DisCos (Distribution Companies) to add 4 million new connections
policy_promises.note: Obligation under privatization contracts with BPE

policy_promises.promise: Connect 1.1 million additional rural households per year
Establish a transparent, competitive Rural Electrification Fund (REF) for capital subsidies
Rural Electrification Agency (REA) to maintain 6 Zonal offices nationwide
Reliable and affordable electricity for all Nigerians regardless of location
Promote cheaper, cleaner alternatives to kerosene, candles, and fossil fuel generators in rural areas
Subsidies focused on expanding access rather than consumption — benefiting the poorest rural households

admitted_failures.finding: Rural electricity access was approximately 26% in 2016 — no significant improvement from when the Rural Electrification Programme began in 1981
Over 70 million rural Nigerians lacked access to reliable electricity supply
Approximately 1,600 rural electrification projects were abandoned across all states as of 2012; contractors had left sites for more than 5 years
Many completed rural electrification projects could not be put to meaningful use because they had no reliable source of power supply — infrastructure built but non-functional
The Rural Electrification Agency (REA) was suspended in late 2009, stalling strategy development for years and explaining why a plan initiated in the early 2000s was only being published in 2016
Population growth outpaced new connections — the rate of new household formation exceeded the rate of new electricity connections
DisCos' commercial objectives led them to focus on urban areas, not rural communities, despite contractual rural obligations
Most abandoned projects were grid extension projects. Even when funded, they relied on a national grid that itself lacked reliable generation
The EPSR Act 2005 established the legal framework for rural electrification but contained 'few details on its implementation' — leaving a gap between legislation and action for over a decade
Rural households continued to rely on fuel-wood, kerosene, candles, and fossil fuel generators — the same energy sources the programme was designed to replace since 1981

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Private sector would drive rural electrification through DisCos and new market entrants
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: DisCos focused commercially on urban areas where returns were higher; rural areas remained unserved

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: The REF would provide transparent, competitive capital subsidies from 2015
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: REA was suspended in 2009; REF was not operational; the 2016 document is still describing the fund as future intent

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: 4 million DisCo connections by 2017 would expand rural access
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: DisCo commercial incentives directed investment to urban, not rural, areas

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Grid extension would bring electricity to rural Local Government Areas
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: 1,600 projects abandoned; completed projects had no reliable power supply

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Off-grid and mini-grid solutions would serve remote communities
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: No viable financing ecosystem; high barriers to entry; capacity gaps among developers
Rural access rate was still ~26% in 2016, unchanged from 1981

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Rural households would transition from kerosene and fuel-wood to grid or off-grid electricity
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: Rural households continued burning kerosene, candles, and fuel-wood — at expense to health, income, and environment

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: The required 6,000MW of new generation for rural demand could be mobilised
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: 6,000MW exceeded the entire existing capacity of the Nigerian power system at the time

documents.ai_narrative: The 2016 RESIP is both a plan and an inadvertent confession. Nigeria's government admitted in writing that 35 years of rural electrification effort produced near-zero measurable improvement. The programme started in 1981. By 2016, the rural access rate was still approximately 26%. Meanwhile, 1,600 projects sat abandoned nationwide, and the very agency meant to fix the problem — the REA — had been suspended in 2009. The new plan required spending more than the entire capacity of the existing Nigerian power system just to hit its 2020 interim target. Rural consumers in 2016 were still burning firewood and kerosene — the same fuels the 1981 programme was designed to replace. The core failure was structural: grid extension as a strategy relied on a national grid that itself could not generate or deliver reliable power. Policy made promises the infrastructure could not keep.
documents.consumer_experience_summary: Rural Nigerians in 2016 experienced darkness, dependence on expensive and unhealthy alternatives (kerosene, candles, fuel-wood, generators), reduced economic productivity, compromised health and education outcomes, and continued migration to urban areas — all outcomes the 1981 Rural Electrification Programme and the 2005/2009 RE Policy explicitly promised to eliminate.

documents.title: Energizing Economies Programme Overview — Upscaling Minigrids for Least Cost and Timely Access to Electricity
documents.category: Commercial/Urban Energy Programme — Baseline Survey and Field Evidence
programme_objectives: Provide safe, reliable and predictable energy to industries and businesses to improve economic output
Create an incentive and working model for private investors in innovative power solutions
Provide street lighting to enable longer and safer business hours

target_sites.name: Ariaria International Market
target_sites.proposed_solution: 5 MW CNG gas fired power plant with independent distribution network and street lighting

target_sites.name: Sabon Gari Market
target_sites.proposed_solution: Stand-alone solar systems (1–2 KW per shop) or 1 MW solar hybrid power plant with street lighting

target_sites.name: Somolu Printing Community
target_sites.note_other: 21 MW is printing equipment — critical productive load
target_sites.proposed_solution: 3–5 MW CNG natural gas fired power plant with independent distribution network and street lighting

target_sites.name: Sura Shopping Complex
target_sites.proposed_solution: Connection to existing gas fired power plant on Lagos Island with independent distribution network and street lighting

status_quo_conditions.condition: 72–84% of traders across all four markets were using alternatives to DisCo (grid) power — the majority had effectively abandoned the national grid
Market traders collectively paying over $70,000 per day for self-generated diesel power across the four markets
status_quo_conditions.consumer_impact: direct financial burden on small and micro businesses

status_quo_conditions.condition: 480+ diesel generators documented running in markets — causing noise pollution and Health, Safety, and Environment (HSE) risks from ad hoc installations
Old and dangerous overhead distribution lines documented at market sites — infrastructure degradation compounding unreliability
Darkness after business hours linked to robbery, harassment, and vandalism — crime directly attributable to lack of reliable street lighting and power
43–59% of traders stated they would expand their shops or hire more staff if given reliable power — demonstrating suppressed economic growth directly caused by energy failure
Somolu Printing Community required 21 MW for printing equipment alone — meaning Nigeria's printing industry was running almost entirely on diesel at enormous cost and carbon output

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: DisCos would serve commercial and industrial clusters reliably after privatization
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: 72–84% of market traders had abandoned DisCo power and were self-generating with diesel

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Privatization of DisCos would lower energy costs and improve reliability for businesses
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: Businesses paying >$70,000 per day collectively for generator power with zero government subsidy

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Grid-connected electricity would support Nigerian commerce and industry
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: Commercial clusters running 480+ diesel generators as primary power — grid was effectively irrelevant to daily business operations

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Urban and peri-urban consumers would benefit from DisCo service improvement
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: Even large, commercially active urban markets (Lagos, Kano, Abia) with high willingness to pay had been abandoned by DisCos

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Economic growth and job creation would follow improved electricity access
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: 43–59% of traders explicitly said they were holding back on expansion and hiring due to energy unreliability — growth was being actively suppressed

policy_vs_reality_gaps.policy_assumption: Safety and quality of life would improve as electricity access expanded
policy_vs_reality_gaps.consumer_reality: Darkness from grid failure directly enabling robbery, harassment, and vandalism in major commercial markets

cross_document_links.note: DOC_001 (RESIP 2016) promised DisCos would add 4 million connections by 2017; DOC_002 shows that one year into that deadline, 72–84% of commercial traders had abandoned DisCo power entirely
DOC_001 described subsidies for consumers and affordable electricity for businesses; DOC_002 shows businesses bearing 100% of energy costs through expensive diesel self-generation

documents.ai_narrative: By 2017, the government's own field surveys confirmed what every Nigerian market trader already knew: the grid had effectively ceased to function as a business utility. In four of Nigeria's most economically active commercial clusters — Ariaria, Sabon Gari, Somolu, Sura — between 72% and 84% of traders had given up on DisCo power entirely and were self-generating with diesel. These markets collectively housed over 50,000 shops and were spending more than $70,000 every day just to keep the lights on. The noise, fumes, and fire risk of hundreds of generators running simultaneously had become the normal operating environment for Nigerian commerce. The Energizing Economies programme was a tacit government admission: the grid could not be fixed fast enough, so off-grid minigrids would have to substitute for it in Nigeria's economic heartland. The 43–59% of traders who said they would expand their businesses if given reliable power represents the starkest measure of how much economic output Nigeria was losing every single day.
documents.consumer_experience_summary: In 2017, Nigeria's commercial consumers — market traders, printers, shop owners in some of the country's largest urban markets — were bearing the full cost of grid failure through daily diesel expenditure, health risks from generator fumes, restricted business hours due to darkness, crime enabled by inadequate lighting, and suppressed economic expansion. These were not rural households — they were the urban commercial engine of the Nigerian economy. And they had been abandoned by the grid.
related_documents: REA Energizing Economies Baseline Survey and Energy Audit Report, 2017
